trade.summary: The EAU supplies crude oil and LNG to Corea del Sud, one of the world's largest energy importers. Corea del Sud exports ships, automobiles, electronics, steel, and construction services. Corea del Sudn construction companies (Samsung, Hyundai) have won massive EAU infrastructure contracts including the Barakah nuclear plant.

diplomatic.summary: The EAU-Corea del Sud Special Strategic Partnership reflects deep ties anchored in the Barakah nuclear plant project (the Arab world's first nuclear power station, built by KEPCO). Corea del Sudn companies have been major contractors in EAU infrastructure for decades. High-level exchanges are regular and frequent.

military.summary: Corea del Sud deploys the Akh Unit — a special forces unit — to the EAU for training EAU special operations forces, a unique arrangement in Corea del Sudn military diplomacy. Corea del Sud has sold military equipment including K9 howitzers and surveillance systems to the EAU. Defense ties are unusually strong for a non-NATO partner.

financial.summary: Corea del Sud National Oil Corporation (KNOC) holds equity stakes in EAU upstream fields. Corea del Sudn banks have EAU operations. EAU sovereign funds have invested in Corea del Sudn equities and bonds. The Barakah nuclear project (approx. $25B) represents one of the largest bilateral economic engagements.

energy.summary: Corea del Sud imports approximately 12% of its crude oil from the EAU. The Barakah nuclear plant (4 APR-1400 reactors, full capacity ~5.6GW) is a landmark project. KEPCO signed a 60-year operating agreement. LNG imports from EAU are growing as Corea del Sud shifts its energy mix.

scientific.summary: Strong collaboration in nuclear technology, construction engineering, and advanced manufacturing. KEPCO's nuclear technology transfer to EAU forms the basis of an unprecedented scientific partnership. Corea del Sudn universities have partnerships with EAU institutions in engineering and technology.
